title: Determinants, Inverses & Systems
blurb: The determinant ad − bc, the 2×2 inverse, solving systems with matrices and Cramer's rule, and matrices as transformations — the capstone.
buildsOn: Matrix multiplication (Unit 5) and the transformation matrices from the Geometry course.

lessons.title: The Determinant
lessons.concreteComparison: Every 2×2 matrix hides one number that answers three questions at once: does the matrix have an inverse, by how much does it scale areas, and do its columns point along one line? That number is the determinant, $ad - bc$ — criss-cross multiply, subtract.
lessons.objective: Compute 2×2 determinants, interpret det as the area scale factor, and use det = 0 as the singularity/parallel-columns test.
concept: For $A = \begin{pmatrix} a & b \\ c & d \end{pmatrix}$: $$\det A = ad - bc$$ — main diagonal product minus anti-diagonal product. One criss-cross, one number.

**Geometric meaning**: any shape transformed by $A$ has its area multiplied by $|\det A|$. Determinant $6$ → areas grow sixfold; determinant $\pm 1$ → rigid (rotations, reflections); a NEGATIVE sign means the plane got mirrored.

$\det A = 0$ is the death certificate: the columns are parallel (one line's worth of directions), all areas collapse to zero, and — next lesson's headline — no inverse exists. Notice the connection to Unit 1: $ad - bc = 0$ is exactly the cross-test for the columns $(a, c)$ and $(b, d)$ being parallel.
lessons.keyIdea: det = ad − bc: the area scale factor (sign = orientation flip); det = 0 ⇔ parallel columns ⇔ no inverse.

facts.title: The formula
facts.latex: \det\begin{pmatrix} a & b \\ c & d \end{pmatrix} = ad - bc

facts.title: Area factor
facts.explanation: Negative determinant = mirrored orientation, same area scale.

workedExamples.statement: A triangle of area $9$ is transformed by $M = \begin{pmatrix} 2 & 1 \\ 3 & 4 \end{pmatrix}$. Find the image's area.
For which $k$ is $\begin{pmatrix} 2 & k \\ 3 & 6 \end{pmatrix}$ singular (determinant zero)?
workedExamples.solution: $12 - 3k = 0$, so $k = 4$. Check the columns at $k = 4$: $(2, 3)$ and $(4, 6)$ — parallel, as singularity demands.

commonMistakes.text: Adding instead of subtracting: ad + bc.
commonMistakes.correction: The anti-diagonal SUBTRACTS: ad − bc. With the plus sign, determinant-zero tests and inverses all silently break.

commonMistakes.text: Forgetting the absolute value for areas.
commonMistakes.correction: det = −3 scales areas by 3, not by −3 — the sign reports orientation (a mirror happened), never negative area.

tryIt.statement: For which $m$ does $\begin{pmatrix} m & 3 \\ 4 & m \end{pmatrix}$ have determinant zero?

steps.title: The criss-cross
steps.body: $$\det\begin{pmatrix} a & b \\ c & d \end{pmatrix} = ad - bc$$ Main diagonal times, anti-diagonal times, subtract. Ten seconds of arithmetic — and it answers whether the matrix is invertible, how areas scale, and whether the columns collapsed onto one line.

steps.title: Area, orientation, life-or-death
steps.body: Reading 1: areas scale by $|\det|$ — the geometry course's transformation-matrices lesson planted this. Reading 2: a negative determinant means the plane got MIRRORED. Reading 3: $\det = 0$ means the columns are parallel — the plane squashes onto a line, areas die, and no inverse can ever undo it.

steps.title: Read the sign
options: doubles areas and mirrors the plane
steps.explanation: Area factor $|-2| = 2$; the negative sign reports an orientation flip — somewhere inside lives a reflection.

steps.title: det = 0 questions are parallel questions
steps.body: "For which k is the matrix singular?" is Unit 1's parallel test in a trench coat: set ad − bc = 0 and you're cross-multiplying the columns. Same reflex, new vocabulary.

steps.title: Determinants came first
steps.body: Determinants were studied by Seki in Japan and Leibniz in Europe in the 1680s — 170 years BEFORE matrices got their name. The number was so useful that the grid was invented to hold it.

lessons.title: The Inverse Matrix
lessons.concreteComparison: Every do has an undo — if you're lucky. The matrix that undoes $A$ is its inverse $A^{-1}$: apply both and you've done nothing, $AA^{-1} = I$. For 2×2's there's a closed recipe — swap, negate, divide by the determinant — and the determinant decides your luck: $\det = 0$ means no undo exists.
lessons.objective: Compute 2×2 inverses via the swap-negate-divide recipe, verify with AA⁻¹ = I, and use det ≠ 0 as the existence test.
concept: The recipe: $$A^{-1} = \frac{1}{ad - bc}\begin{pmatrix} d & -b \\ -c & a \end{pmatrix}$$ — SWAP the main diagonal, NEGATE the anti-diagonal, DIVIDE by the determinant.
It exists exactly when $\det A \ne 0$. A singular matrix squashes the plane onto a line — information is destroyed, and no matrix can un-destroy it.

Verification is one multiplication: $AA^{-1}$ must equal $I$ (and $A^{-1}A = I$ too — inverses are the exception to order-anxiety). On exams, always run the check: it converts an inverse from 'hopefully right' to 'proven right' in fifteen seconds.
lessons.keyIdea: A⁻¹ = (1/det)·(swap diagonal, negate anti-diagonal); exists ⇔ det ≠ 0; verify with AA⁻¹ = I.

facts.title: Verification
facts.explanation: The definition — and your fifteen-second proof.

workedExamples.solution: $\det A = 6 - 5 = 1$. Swap-negate-divide: $A^{-1} = \begin{pmatrix} 2 & -5 \\ -1 & 3 \end{pmatrix}$. Verify one cell of $AA^{-1}$: $3 \cdot 2 + 5 \cdot (-1) = 1$ ✓.

workedExamples.statement: Does $C = \begin{pmatrix} 2 & 6 \\ 3 & 9 \end{pmatrix}$ have an inverse? Solve, then explain geometrically.
workedExamples.solution: $\det C = 18 - 18 = 0$ — no inverse. Geometrically: the columns $(2,3)$ and $(6,9)$ are parallel, so $C$ flattens the whole plane onto that line; the flattening can't be undone.

commonMistakes.text: Swapping the anti-diagonal and negating the main one.
commonMistakes.correction: SWAP a and d (main diagonal); NEGATE b and c (anti-diagonal). Run AA⁻¹ = I once and any mix-up confesses immediately.

commonMistakes.text: Forgetting to divide by the determinant.
commonMistakes.correction: Without the 1/det factor, AA⁻¹ = det·I, not I. If det = 1 you got lucky; any other det and the answer is scaled garbage.

steps.title: AA⁻¹ = I
steps.body: The inverse of $A$ is the matrix that undoes it: apply $A$, then $A^{-1}$, and every point is back home — $AA^{-1} = I$. Solving matrix equations, decoding transformations, cracking systems: all of it runs through the inverse.

steps.title: Swap, negate, divide
steps.body: $$A^{-1} = \frac{1}{ad - bc}\begin{pmatrix} d & -b \\ -c & a \end{pmatrix}$$ Swap the main diagonal, negate the anti-diagonal, divide everything by the determinant. And the determinant is the gatekeeper: $\det = 0$ → the plane was squashed onto a line → nothing can unsquash it → no inverse.
beats: swap a↔d, negate b and c

steps.title: Before dividing
steps.explanation: Swap $5$ and $2$, negate the $3$'s, divide by $1$. Quick verify: $5 \cdot 2 + 3 \cdot (-3) = 1$ ✓.

steps.title: The gatekeeper
steps.explanation: $3 \cdot 4 - 6 \cdot 2 = 0$ — singular. (Its columns $(3,2)$ and $(6,4)$ are parallel.) The others have det $3, -1$… alive.

steps.title: Spend 15 seconds on AA⁻¹
steps.body: After computing an inverse, multiply back — just the top-left cell if you're rushed: it must be 1. Every swap/negate/divide slip gets caught by that single number.

steps.title: Bigger matrices, same story
steps.body: 3×3 and beyond have inverses too — computed by Gaussian elimination rather than a neat recipe. But the gatekeeper never changes: det ≠ 0, in every dimension, forever.

steps.title: What sticks
points: Swap main diagonal, negate anti-diagonal, divide by det.
Verify: $AA^{-1} = I$ — fifteen seconds, total certainty.

lessons.title: Solving Systems with Matrices
lessons.concreteComparison: The system $\begin{cases} 3x + 5y = 1 \\ x + 2y = 0 \end{cases}$ is secretly one matrix equation: $A\binom{x}{y} = \binom{1}{0}$. And matrix equations have a one-move solution: multiply both sides by $A^{-1}$. Two equations, two unknowns — one inverse.
lessons.objective: Rewrite 2×2 systems as AX = B, solve via X = A⁻¹B, and use Cramer's rule as the determinant shortcut.
concept: Any 2×2 linear system packs into $AX = B$: coefficients in $A$, unknowns in the column $X$, constants in $B$. Multiply both sides by $A^{-1}$ (on the LEFT — order is sacred): $$X = A^{-1}B.$$

**Cramer's rule** is the same solution with the inverse pre-chewed into determinants: $$x = \frac{\det A_x}{\det A}, \qquad y = \frac{\det A_y}{\det A},$$ where $A_x$ is $A$ with its FIRST column replaced by $B$, and $A_y$ replaces the second. Three criss-crosses and you're done.

$\det A = 0$ is the degenerate flag: the system has no unique solution — either none (parallel lines) or infinitely many (the same line twice). The determinant you learned two lessons ago is exactly the 'does this system behave?' test.
lessons.keyIdea: System = AX = B; solve X = A⁻¹B, or Cramer: x = det Aₓ/det A, y = det A_y/det A; det = 0 flags no-unique-solution.

facts.title: Matrix form
facts.explanation: Multiply by the inverse on the left.

facts.title: Cramer's rule
facts.latex: x = \frac{\det A_x}{\det A},\; y = \frac{\det A_y}{\det A}
facts.explanation: Replace a column with B, criss-cross, divide.

facts.title: Degenerate
facts.explanation: Parallel or identical lines — the determinant warns first.

workedExamples.statement: Solve by Cramer's rule: $\begin{cases} 2x + y = 7 \\ x - 3y = -7 \end{cases}$
For which $k$ does $\begin{cases} 2x + 3y = 5 \\ 4x + ky = 7 \end{cases}$ FAIL to have a unique solution?
workedExamples.solution: $\det A = 2k - 12 = 0$ at $k = 6$. There the left sides are proportional ($4x + 6y = 2(2x + 3y)$) but the right sides aren't ($7 \ne 10$): parallel lines, no solution at all.

commonMistakes.text: Multiplying by A⁻¹ on the wrong side.
commonMistakes.correction: AX = B needs A⁻¹ on the LEFT of both sides: X = A⁻¹B. Writing BA⁻¹ is a different product (when it even exists) — order never stopped mattering.

commonMistakes.text: Replacing the wrong column in Cramer's rule.
commonMistakes.correction: For x, replace the x-COLUMN (first) with B; for y, the second. Swapping gives the two answers exchanged — plausible-looking and wrong.

tryIt.statement: Solve by Cramer: $\begin{cases} x + y = 5 \\ 2x - y = 1 \end{cases}$
For which $m$ does $\begin{cases} 3x + my = 1 \\ 6x + 8y = 5 \end{cases}$ lose its unique solution?

steps.title: Two equations, one equation
steps.body: $\begin{cases} 3x + 5y = 1 \\ x + 2y = 0 \end{cases}$ is $\begin{pmatrix} 3 & 5 \\ 1 & 2 \end{pmatrix}\binom{x}{y} = \binom{1}{0}$ — coefficients, unknowns, constants: $AX = B$. One matrix equation instead of two number equations, and matrix tools now apply.

steps.title: Two lines, one crossing
steps.teach: Each equation is a line; the solution is where they cross. Steer the second line: when the slopes match, the crossing escapes to infinity — that's exactly when the coefficient matrix's determinant hits zero. Algebra's det = 0 and geometry's parallel lines are the same event.

steps.title: Inverse, or Cramer
steps.body: Solver 1: $X = A^{-1}B$ — one inverse, one multiplication. Solver 2, **Cramer's rule**: $x = \frac{\det A_x}{\det A}$, $y = \frac{\det A_y}{\det A}$, where $A_x$ swaps $B$ into the first column, $A_y$ into the second. Three criss-crosses total — the exam speedrun.
beats: X = A⁻¹B — inverse on the LEFT
Cramer: replace a column with B, divide dets

steps.title: Pack it
steps.prompt: The system $\begin{cases} 4x - y = 6 \\ 2x + 3y = 8 \end{cases}$ has coefficient matrix…
steps.explanation: Rows mirror the equations, signs included: $(4, -1)$ then $(2, 3)$. (The second option is its transpose — the classic repack error.)

steps.title: Substitute the answer back
steps.body: Whatever solver you used, plug (x, y) into the ORIGINAL equations. Ten seconds converts 'probably right' into 'checked' — and exams punish unchecked Cramer sign slips mercilessly.

steps.title: Cramer's rule doesn't scale
steps.body: Cramer published his rule in 1750, and it's lovely for 2×2 — but for a 20-variable system it needs more arithmetic than there are grains of sand on Earth. Real solvers use elimination; Cramer survives as the exam's pocket knife.

steps.title: What sticks
points: Pack: $AX = B$; solve: $X = A^{-1}B$ (inverse on the left).
Cramer: replace a column with $B$, divide determinants.
$\det A = 0$: parallel/identical lines — no unique solution.

lessons.title: Matrices as Transformations — the Capstone
lessons.concreteComparison: Everything converges here: a matrix IS a transformation of the plane (its columns say where the unit points go), multiplication IS composition (do one, then the other), the determinant IS the area report, and the inverse IS the undo. One object, four readings — and every exam question about matrices lives in one of them.
lessons.objective: Move fluently between the four readings: matrix as transformation, product as composition, determinant as area factor, inverse as undo.
concept: **Columns = destinations.** The matrix's first column is where $(1, 0)$ lands, the second is where $(0, 1)$ lands. Build any transformation's matrix by asking the two unit points where they go — the geometry course's transformation-matrices lesson, now with full machinery behind it.

**Product = composition, right-to-left.** $BA$ means 'apply $A$ first, then $B$' — the matrix nearest the point acts first. Non-commutativity is just the fact that order of operations matters in geometry.

**Determinant = area factor; inverse = undo.** $|\det|$ prices areas, its sign reports mirroring; $A^{-1}$ reverses the map (and exists only when nothing was flattened). Compositions multiply determinants: $\det(BA) = \det B \cdot \det A$ — undo-ability is inherited or lost at each stage.
lessons.keyIdea: Columns = where (1,0),(0,1) go; BA = A then B; det multiplies under composition; inverse = the undo when det ≠ 0.

facts.title: Columns
facts.explanation: Build and decode every matrix through two points.

facts.title: Composition
facts.explanation: Nearest the point acts first — read right to left.

workedExamples.statement: Build the matrix that rotates $90°$ counter-clockwise and then doubles every length.
workedExamples.solution: Track the unit points: $(1,0) \to (0,1) \to (0,2)$; $(0,1) \to (-1,0) \to (-2,0)$. Columns up: $\begin{pmatrix} 0 & -2 \\ 2 & 0 \end{pmatrix}$ — equivalently $2I \cdot R = \begin{pmatrix} 2 & 0 \\ 0 & 2 \end{pmatrix}\begin{pmatrix} 0 & -1 \\ 1 & 0 \end{pmatrix}$.

workedExamples.statement: $A$ reflects over the $x$-axis, $B$ rotates $90°$ CCW. Compute $BA$ and $AB$ applied to $(1, 0)$ — same?
workedExamples.solution: $BA$: reflect first — $(1,0) \to (1,0)$ — then rotate: $(0, 1)$. $AB$: rotate first — $(1,0) \to (0,1)$ — then reflect: $(0, -1)$. Different! Order changed the destination, which is exactly $AB \ne BA$ in pictures.

workedExamples.statement: $T = \begin{pmatrix} 3 & 1 \\ 2 & 4 \end{pmatrix}$ maps a square of area $2$; then $S$ with $\det S = \tfrac{1}{2}$ is applied. Find the final area, and decide whether the combined map is invertible.
workedExamples.solution: $\det T = 12 - 2 = 10$: area $2 \to 20$. Then $\times \tfrac{1}{2}$: final area $10$. Combined determinant $= \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot 10 = 5 \ne 0$ — invertible; the undo exists.

commonMistakes.text: Reading BA as 'B first, then A'.
commonMistakes.correction: The matrix NEAREST the point acts first: BA(x) = B(A(x)) — A first. Right-to-left, like function composition, because that's what it is.
Area factors MULTIPLY: det(BA) = det B · det A. A doubling followed by a tripling scales by 6, not 5.

tryIt.statement: What single matrix scales by $3$ and then rotates $180°$?
$\det A = 4$ and $\det B = -2$. Find $\det(BA)$, the combined area factor, and whether orientation flips.
tryIt.solution: $\det(BA) = -8$: areas scale by $8$, and the negative sign means one mirror happened — orientation flips.

steps.title: One object, four readings
steps.body: A matrix is simultaneously: a TRANSFORMATION (columns = where the unit points land), a COMPOSER (multiplication chains maps, right-to-left), an AREA ACCOUNTANT (determinant), and — when $\det \ne 0$ — an UNDOABLE act (inverse). Every exam question picks one reading; fluency means switching between them mid-problem.

steps.title: A turn you can decode
steps.teach: This $90°$ rotation sends $(1,0) \to (0,1)$ and $(0,1) \to (-1,0)$ — stand those up as columns and you've rebuilt $\begin{pmatrix} 0 & -1 \\ 1 & 0 \end{pmatrix}$, with $\det = 1$: rigid, orientation kept, undo = rotate back ($R^{-1} = R^3$).

steps.title: Right to left
steps.explanation: $BA(x) = B(A(x))$ — the matrix touching the point goes first. Function composition notation, inherited.

steps.title: Compose the areas
steps.prompt: Map 1 has $\det = 3$, map 2 has $\det = -1$. After both, a shape of area $4$ has area…
steps.explanation: Determinants multiply: $3 \cdot (-1) = -3$; areas take the absolute value: $4 \cdot 3 = 12$ (mirrored once).

steps.title: Name the reading first
steps.body: Before computing, say which reading the question wants: destination of a point (columns), combined map (product), area (det), or undo (inverse). Half of matrix-exam errors are answering in the wrong reading.

steps.title: You've built linear algebra
steps.body: Vectors, dot products, matrices, determinants, inverses, transformations — assembled together, these are the first chapter of linear algebra, the mathematics running graphics, machine learning, quantum mechanics, and Google's original search ranking. This course was the front door.

steps.title: What sticks
points: Columns say where $(1,0)$ and $(0,1)$ land — build and decode.
$\det \ne 0$ ⇔ the undo exists — the course's through-line.

practice.statement: For which $k$ is $\begin{pmatrix} k & 8 \\ 2 & k \end{pmatrix}$ singular?
A shape of area $7$ is transformed by a matrix with determinant $-3$. Find the image's area.
For which $m$ does $\begin{cases} 2x + 5y = 3 \\ 4x + my = 1 \end{cases}$ have NO unique solution?
practice.solution: $2m - 20 = 0$: $m = 10$ (and there the right sides break proportionality: no solution).

testYourself.solution: $36 - 36 = 0$ — singular: columns parallel, the plane flattens, no inverse.

testYourself.statement: Find the inverse of $\begin{pmatrix} 7 & 4 \\ 5 & 3 \end{pmatrix}$ and verify the top-left entry of $AA^{-1}$.
A map first scales areas by $3$, then a second map leaves the total area factor at $12$. What is the second map's $|\det|$? If the first mirrored and the second didn't, does the composite mirror?
testYourself.solution: $|\det_2| = 12/3 = 4$. One mirror total → the composite IS mirrored (composite det negative).

testYourself.statement: Build the single matrix for: reflect over the $x$-axis, then rotate $90°$ CCW.
testYourself.solution: $(1,0) \to (1,0) \to (0,1)$; $(0,1) \to (0,-1) \to (1,0)$. Matrix: $\begin{pmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ 1 & 0 \end{pmatrix}$ — reflection over $y = x$! Two maps composed into one familiar face, $\det = -1$.

testYourself.statement: For which $k$ does $\begin{pmatrix} 1 & 2 \\ 3 & k \end{pmatrix}$ have an inverse, and what is it (in terms of $k$) at $k = 8$?
